disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The current crash followed clear over-exertion two days earlier, on a background of a two-year post-infectious 'wiped-out' illness that began after a documented Q fever infection.
trigger_question_examples: What was happening in the days before this crash started?
Has he had any long-standing health problems or illnesses?
Did anything seem to set this off?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's had this exhaustion thing for about two years, ever since he caught Q fever from work. He gets these crashes when he overdoes it — two days ago he pushed himself too hard and now he's completely flattened.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The weakness and slowing are symmetric and whole-body; carer reports no unilateral facial droop, no one-sided limb weakness, and speech is slow/quiet but not slurred (pertinent negative screen for an acute focal neurological event).
trigger_question_examples: Is the weakness or drooping on one side of his face or body, or is it even on both sides?
Are his words slurred, or just slow and quiet?
Can he move both arms and both legs equally, and does his smile look even?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, it's the same on both sides — both arms and legs are just as heavy and weak. His face looks even, he's not drooping, and his words aren't slurred, just really slow and faint.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: He's just weak all over and hard to understand because he's so wiped out.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No fever, rigors, night sweats, new breathlessness or chest pain (pertinent negative screen for acute infection relapse / chronic Q fever endocarditis).
trigger_question_examples: Has he had any fever, shakes, or drenching night sweats?
Any new chest pain or breathlessness?
Have you been able to check his temperature?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No fever — I checked and it was normal, 36.6. No shakes or sweats, and he hasn't complained of any chest pain or trouble breathing.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Markedly reduced oral intake and near-absent urine output because he is too exhausted to drink unaided; bedbound and fully dependent on his spouse (dehydration risk).
trigger_question_examples: How is he going with drinking and eating?
Has he been passing urine normally?
Is he able to look after himself at all, or do you have to help him?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's barely drinking — I have to hold the cup for him — and he's hardly passed any urine. He can't do anything on his own, I'm helping him with everything.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Home-obtainable vitals available and reassuring: BP 110/70, HR 68, SpO2 98%, afebrile — patient/carer can offer these on request.
trigger_question_examples: Do you have a blood pressure cuff or oximeter at home you could use?
Can you take his blood pressure, pulse or oxygen level for me?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, I've got a home monitor — his blood pressure is 110/70, pulse is 68, and his oxygen is reading 98%.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The exhaustion is completely unrefreshed by rest or sleep, and effort of any kind (including talking or moving) worsens it — post-exertional, sleep-unresponsive pattern.
trigger_question_examples: Does resting or sleeping help at all?
What makes the exhaustion better or worse?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Sleep and rest don't help him at all — if anything, any effort like talking or moving makes it worse. He just stays flat.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Do you think we need to get him to hospital tonight? Last time someone came out to us — but the trip really knocks him around and makes the crash so much worse.
